title: Cooperation and Conflict on the Cape Frontier
subject: History
caps_topic: Cooperation and conflict on the frontiers of the Cape Colony in the early 19th century

meta.title: Cooperation and Conflict on the Cape Frontier
meta.subject: History
meta.caps_topic: Cooperation and conflict on the frontiers of the Cape Colony in the early 19th century

sources.title: Grade 7 - Term 4: Co-operation and conflict on the frontiers of the Cape Colony in the early 19th century
Contact, conflict and dispossession on the Cape Eastern (or Northern) frontiers in the nineteenth century
Early struggles: contact and conflict at the Cape Colony
The establishment of the Cape and its impact on the Khoikhoi and Dutch
The role of missionaries in the conquest
European missionaries in southern Africa: the role of missionaries
Fifth Frontier War: the sangoma Makana attacks Grahamstown
Xhosa King Hintsa ka Phalo killed by British troops

slides.title: Cooperation and Conflict on the Cape Frontier
slides.lead: How Xhosa communities, trekboers, the British and missionaries met, traded and clashed on the Eastern Cape frontier in the early 1800s.

media.alt: A green river winds between two landscapes, one dotted with cattle and one with a wagon, meeting at a boundary.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='118' width='320' height='62' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M0 118 L60 96 L120 112 L180 90 L250 110 L320 92 L320 118 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M150 40 q10 40 -6 70 q-14 26 4 70' fill='none' stroke='#4a6d8c' stroke-width='7' stroke-linecap='round'/><circle cx='250' cy='44' r='22' fill='#e8a13a'/><ellipse cx='60' cy='138' rx='12' ry='7' fill='#5c3a22'/><rect x='214' y='128' width='34' height='14' fill='#7a5a3a'/><circle cx='220' cy='146' r='5' fill='#3a2a1a'/><circle cx='242' cy='146' r='5' fill='#3a2a1a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Set the scene: a 'frontier' is not just a line on a map but a wide zone where different peoples live close together, sometimes helping each other and sometimes fighting. Explain that this unit looks at the Eastern Cape frontier in the early 1800s, roughly along the Great Fish River. Ask learners what they think happens when two groups both want the same land and water.

slides.title: The Eastern Cape frontier
slides.lead: By 1800 the Cape Colony had spread east until Dutch farmers met Xhosa communities near the Great Fish River.

blocks.text: The **Cape Colony** began as a Dutch settlement at Table Bay in 1652. Over 150 years, cattle farmers called **trekboers** moved further and further east looking for grazing land. In 1795, and again in 1806, **Britain** took control of the Cape.

items: The frontier zone lay in today's Eastern Cape, around the Great Fish River.
It was not a fixed border - it shifted as people moved and wars were fought.
Different groups shared, competed for and fought over the same land and water.

media.alt: A simple map showing the Cape Colony in the west and Xhosa land in the east, divided by the Great Fish River.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='10' y='20' width='104' height='120' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='126' y='20' width='104' height='120' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M120 18 q-8 40 4 62 q10 22 -4 62' fill='none' stroke='#4a6d8c' stroke-width='6'/><text x='30' y='84' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#3a2a1a'>Cape</text><text x='24' y='98' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#3a2a1a'>Colony</text><text x='150' y='84' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#243318'>Xhosa</text><text x='152' y='98' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#243318'>land</text></svg>

slides.notes: Locate the frontier in space and time for learners. Point on a map to the Eastern Cape and the Great Fish River. Stress that the border kept moving eastward as the colony grew. Note the change from Dutch to British control, which mattered because Britain brought new settlers and new policies to the frontier.

slides.title: Who lived on the frontier?
slides.lead: Several groups shared the frontier zone, each with their own way of life.

blocks.heading: Xhosa communities
blocks.text: Cattle-keeping farmers living in chiefdoms east of the Fish River, led by chiefs such as Ngqika and Ndlambe.

blocks.heading: Trekboers
blocks.text: Dutch-speaking cattle farmers from the Cape who moved east in search of new grazing land.

blocks.heading: Khoikhoi and San
blocks.text: The earliest herders and hunters of the region, many now working on colonial farms or living among the Xhosa.

blocks.heading: British and missionaries
blocks.text: British officials, soldiers and 1820 Settlers, plus missionaries who came to teach and convert.

slides.notes: Give learners a cast of characters before the story unfolds. Emphasise that the Xhosa were farmers with a strong social and political system, not simply 'in the way' of settlers. Note that Khoikhoi and San peoples were already there long before either group. Avoid painting any group as all-good or all-bad.

slides.title: Trading and living together
slides.lead: For much of the time, frontier people cooperated because they needed each other.

blocks.text: Life on the frontier was not only about fighting. Xhosa communities and colonists **traded** with one another, swapping cattle, ivory, beads, copper, cloth and metal tools. Some people learned each other's languages, worked together and even married across groups.

items: Cattle were wealth for both Xhosa and trekboer farmers, so they traded and herded side by side.
Khoikhoi workers, hunters and traders often moved between the two societies.
Missionaries, chiefs and officials sometimes made agreements to keep the peace.

media.alt: Two hands exchanging goods above a pair of cattle, showing trade.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M40 60 q30 -20 60 0' fill='none' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='9' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M120 60 q30 -20 60 0' fill='none' stroke='#2f5233' stroke-width='9' stroke-linecap='round'/><circle cx='110' cy='58' r='10' fill='#e8a13a'/><ellipse cx='70' cy='118' rx='22' ry='12' fill='#5c3a22'/><ellipse cx='150' cy='118' rx='22' ry='12' fill='#8a6a44'/><path d='M52 108 q-6 -10 4 -12 M88 108 q6 -10 -4 -12' stroke='#3a2a1a' stroke-width='3' fill='none'/></svg>

slides.notes: This slide balances the story so learners do not think the frontier was only violent. Real relationships - trade, work, marriage, language learning - crossed the frontier. This 'cooperation' is half of the CAPS topic and is easy to overlook. Ask learners for modern examples of groups who both compete and cooperate.

slides.title: Why did conflict grow?
slides.lead: The same things that people shared - land, water and cattle - also caused their quarrels.

blocks.text: As more trekboers and later British settlers arrived, they wanted more **land** and **grazing**. This pushed against Xhosa communities who were already using that land. When cattle went missing, each side blamed the other, and small raids could grow into war.

items: Both groups kept cattle, so competition over grazing land was constant.
Cattle raiding by both sides created anger and mistrust.
The colonial government kept trying to fix a hard border and remove Xhosa from land west of it.

media.alt: A cow at the centre with two arrows pulling in opposite directions, showing competition.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='84' rx='30' ry='18' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M92 70 q-6 -10 4 -12 M128 70 q6 -10 -4 -12' stroke='#3a2a1a' stroke-width='4' fill='none'/><path d='M64 84 L20 84 M28 76 L20 84 L28 92' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='6' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><path d='M156 84 L200 84 M192 76 L200 84 L192 92' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='6' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/></svg>

slides.notes: Draw the cause-and-effect clearly: growing settler numbers plus a shared cattle economy plus a shifting border led to conflict. Point out that cattle raiding went both ways - this is important for fairness. Explain that the government's attempts to draw a firm line, and to keep the Xhosa on one side of it, made peace harder.

slides.title: The Frontier Wars
slides.lead: Between 1779 and 1879 there were a series of wars between colonists and Xhosa communities on the Eastern Cape frontier.

blocks.text: Historians count around **nine Frontier Wars** over about a hundred years. In this unit we look at the ones in the **early 1800s**. Each war usually ended with the Xhosa losing more land, as the colony pushed its border further east.

items: The wars were also called the Cape Frontier Wars or the Xhosa Wars.
They were not one long battle but many separate conflicts, with peace in between.
Over time the pattern was clear: land moved from Xhosa control into the colony.

media.alt: A colony boundary marked by a dashed line stepping further to the right over time.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='10' y='30' width='60' height='100' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='70' y='30' width='40' height='100' fill='#cbb98f' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='110' y='30' width='40' height='100' fill='#cfc094' opacity='0.5'/><line x1='70' y1='24' x2='70' y2='136' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><line x1='110' y1='24' x2='110' y2='136' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><line x1='150' y1='24' x2='150' y2='136' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><path d='M74 20 L146 20 M138 14 L146 20 L138 26' fill='none' stroke='#3a2a1a' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Give the big picture before the details. Learners do not need to memorise all nine wars; they need to grasp the pattern - repeated conflict, each war ending with the frontier pushed east and the Xhosa losing land. [SME] Historians differ on exactly how the nine wars are numbered and dated; confirm the count and dates you want learners to use.

slides.title: Driven from the Zuurveld
slides.lead: British forces pushed thousands of Xhosa across the Fish River to clear land for the colony.

blocks.text: The **Zuurveld** was a stretch of land west of the Fish River where Xhosa and colonists had lived side by side. In 1811-1812, Governor **Sir John Cradock** ordered British troops and their allies to **drive the Xhosa out** and force them east across the river.

items: The colony wanted a clear, empty buffer zone along its eastern edge.
To guard the new border, the town of Grahamstown was founded in 1812.
Many Xhosa families lost homes and grazing land they had used for years.

media.alt: Figures with cattle being moved across a river towards the east.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M112 20 q-8 50 4 62 q10 20 -4 58' fill='none' stroke='#4a6d8c' stroke-width='8'/><g fill='#2f5233'><circle cx='150' cy='60' r='8'/><rect x='144' y='70' width='12' height='26'/><circle cx='178' cy='72' r='8'/><rect x='172' y='82' width='12' height='26'/></g><ellipse cx='196' cy='108' rx='16' ry='9' fill='#5c3a22'/><path d='M150 120 L200 120 M192 114 L200 120 L192 126' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='5'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is a concrete example of dispossession. Explain 'buffer zone' - the colony wanted empty land between itself and the Xhosa. Note the founding of Grahamstown (today Makhanda) as a military frontier town. Handle this sensitively: real families were forced from their land. Ask learners how they think the Xhosa felt about being pushed out.

slides.title: The 1820 British Settlers
slides.lead: Britain sent about 4,000 settlers to live on the frontier as a human shield for the colony.

blocks.text: In 1820 the British government helped around **4,000 settlers** move to the Eastern Cape. They were given farms in the frontier zone, partly to grow the colony and partly to **defend the border** against the Xhosa.

items: Many settlers struggled: the land was hard to farm and conflict was close by.
Some gave up farming and started towns, trades and newspapers instead.
Their arrival added to the pressure on Xhosa land near the frontier.

media.alt: A sailing ship near the coast with a wagon and small farmhouse on the shore.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='104' width='240' height='56' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect y='92' width='240' height='14' fill='#a8bcae'/><g><rect x='40' y='58' width='6' height='40' fill='#5c3a22'/><path d='M46 60 L84 60 L64 96 L46 96 Z' fill='#efe6d2' stroke='#8a6a44'/><path d='M30 96 L96 96 L86 108 L40 108 Z' fill='#7a5a3a'/></g><rect x='150' y='96' width='40' height='24' fill='#b7a98d'/><path d='M148 96 L170 78 L192 96 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='165' y='104' width='10' height='16' fill='#5c3a22'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the British plan: place settlers on the frontier to strengthen and defend it. Note that many settlers had a hard time and turned to town trades, giving rise to Grahamstown's growth and the frontier press. Keep balance: the settlers were often ordinary poor families, but their arrival still increased pressure on Xhosa land.

slides.title: Makhanda and the attack on Grahamstown
slides.lead: In 1819 a Xhosa army led by the war-leader and prophet Makhanda attacked the frontier town of Grahamstown.

blocks.text: The Xhosa were divided between two leaders, **Ngqika** and his uncle **Ndlambe**. When the British backed Ngqika, Ndlambe's forces resisted. In 1819 the warrior-prophet **Makhanda** (also called Nxele or Makana) led a large Xhosa army in a daring attack on **Grahamstown**.

items: The attack failed, and the Xhosa suffered heavy losses.
Makhanda gave himself up and was imprisoned on Robben Island, where he drowned trying to escape.
After the war the colony took even more land, creating a 'neutral' zone the Xhosa could not enter.

media.alt: A single tall figure raising a spear stands before a small walled town.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><g fill='#b7a98d'><rect x='150' y='84' width='70' height='36'/><rect x='160' y='72' width='16' height='12'/><rect x='192' y='72' width='16' height='12'/></g><circle cx='60' cy='52' r='12' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='54' y='66' width='12' height='40' fill='#2f5233'/><line x1='74' y1='30' x2='74' y2='108' stroke='#5c3a22' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M74 30 L70 44 L78 44 Z' fill='#7a7a7a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain the split between Ngqika and Ndlambe and how British involvement deepened the conflict. Present Makhanda as an important leader and prophet, not just an attacker. His imprisonment and death on Robben Island make him an early symbol of resistance. Note again the pattern: after the war, the colony seized more land. [SME] The size of Makhanda's force is often given as around 10,000; verify the figure before quoting it to learners.

slides.title: Helpers and part of the conquest
slides.lead: Missionaries came to teach and convert - but their work also opened the way for colonial control.

items: Built mission stations, schools and churches.
Wrote down the Xhosa language and taught reading and writing.
Sometimes defended African people against harsh treatment.
They pressed people to give up their own beliefs and customs.
Mission stations often became footholds for colonial power.
Their reports and maps helped the colony understand and control the land.

slides.notes: Missionaries are a great example of the 'cooperation and conflict' theme being complicated. Groups like the London Missionary Society (Dr Johannes van der Kemp, later Dr John Philip) sometimes spoke up for Khoikhoi and Xhosa rights, yet mission work also weakened African independence and eased colonial expansion. Encourage learners to hold both ideas at once rather than judging missionaries as simply good or bad.

slides.title: The killing of King Hintsa
slides.lead: In 1835 the British killed the paramount Xhosa king, Hintsa, during a war over land and cattle.

blocks.text: In 1834 war broke out again when Xhosa forces, angry at losing land, swept into the colony. During the fighting the British seized the **paramount king Hintsa ka Phalo**. In May 1835 he was **shot and killed by British troops** while a prisoner, an act that shocked and angered the Xhosa.

items: The war caused great loss of life and cattle on both sides.
Hintsa's death became a lasting symbol of colonial injustice.
The colony again pushed its border eastward after the war.

media.alt: A broken spear laid across a shield, with a low sun behind, marking a leader's death.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M0 118 L240 118 L240 160 L0 160 Z' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='120' cy='118' r='34' fill='#e0913a' opacity='0.6'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='96' rx='26' ry='40' fill='#5c3a22'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='96' rx='10' ry='30' fill='#efe6d2'/><line x1='70' y1='70' x2='104' y2='104' stroke='#7a7a7a' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='138' y1='118' x2='172' y2='150' stroke='#7a7a7a' stroke-width='4'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is a sensitive moment - handle it soberly and factually. Hintsa was the senior Xhosa king; his killing while a captive was widely seen as a betrayal and remains painful history. Focus on why it mattered: it deepened Xhosa resistance and mistrust. Avoid graphic detail; the key point is the injustice and its long shadow. [SME] The exact circumstances of Hintsa's death and mutilation are debated by historians; keep the classroom account careful and confirm wording.

slides.title: A leader who fought to keep the land
slides.lead: Chief Maqoma led Xhosa resistance for decades and died a prisoner on Robben Island.

blocks.text: The land was ours, and we shall never stop feeling that it was taken from us.
blocks.cite: Remembered spirit of Xhosa resistance on the frontier

blocks.text: **Maqoma**, a son of Chief Ngqika, was one of the most skilled Xhosa military leaders of the frontier wars. He was pushed off his land in the Kat River area and spent much of his life resisting colonial expansion. He was later imprisoned on **Robben Island**, where he died in 1873.

items: He used the hills and bush of the frontier cleverly against larger armies.
His life shows the human cost of losing land, again and again.
Today he is remembered as a hero of resistance.

slides.notes: Maqoma gives learners a memorable individual to anchor the theme of resistance and dispossession. Note that the quote is a paraphrase capturing the Xhosa sense of loss, not a verified direct quotation - present it as such. Connect his imprisonment on Robben Island to Makhanda earlier, showing a long line of resistance leaders held there. [SME] Confirm you are comfortable using a paraphrased quote, or replace it with a sourced one.

slides.title: A shifting frontier, 1806-1836

blocks.alt: A timeline of key frontier events from 1806 to 1836.

events.label: Britain takes lasting control of the Cape Colony
Xhosa driven from the Zuurveld; Grahamstown founded
Makhanda attacks Grahamstown; more land taken
British settlers arrive on the frontier
King Hintsa killed during the Sixth Frontier War
Great Trek begins as Boers leave the colony

slides.notes: Use this timeline to consolidate. Ask learners to trace the pattern across the events: each conflict tends to end with the frontier pushed east and the Xhosa losing land. Note the Great Trek at the end, which learners may study next - it grew partly out of frontier tensions and changes in colonial law.

slides.title: What we learned
slides.lead: The Cape frontier was a place of both cooperation and conflict - trade and marriage, but also war and dispossession.

blocks.text: On the Eastern Cape frontier, Xhosa communities, trekboers, Khoikhoi, British settlers and missionaries **shared** the land through trade and daily life, but also **clashed** over land, cattle and power. War after war ended with the Xhosa losing land, while the colony's border moved steadily east.

blocks.question: Give two examples of cooperation between different groups on the Cape frontier.
Explain two reasons why conflict grew on the frontier in the early 1800s.
Choose one leader (Makhanda, Hintsa or Maqoma) and explain why they are remembered.

slides.notes: Close by returning to the two halves of the topic - cooperation and conflict - so learners can hold both in mind. Use the three questions as written work or discussion; the mark allocations follow CAPS-style short-answer and paragraph writing. Remind learners that this frontier history helps explain later events such as the Great Trek and the loss of African land across South Africa.
